title: Happy New Year
description: Happy New Year is a short story collection by Rubem Fonseca. Banned in Brazil during the military dictatorship by order of the then Minister of Justice, Armando Falcão, under the accusation of "attacking morality and good habits".
descriptionParagraphs: Happy New Year is a short story collection by Rubem Fonseca. Banned in Brazil during the military dictatorship by order of the then Minister of Justice, Armando Falcão, under the accusation of "attacking morality and good habits".

Its interest lies in how censors blur depiction, endorsement, and imitation, treating a book's violent material as if it were already an act. As a short story collection, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Brazil. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Banned in Brazil during the military dictatorship by order of the then Minister of Justice, Armando Falcão, under the accusation of "attacking morality and good habits". The author of the book, Rubem Fonseca, filed a. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

banningParagraphs: Happy New Year entered censorship debates as a short story collection associated with risk, sensational culture, and violence. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around incitement to violence and violence.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
